  sahm_gap                      +0.17 pp
  unrate_range_width            +0.80 pp
  unrate_above_12m_low          +0.00 pp

Policy language on this driver — CHANGED since the previous statement
  - The pace of job gains slowed and the unemployment rate held steady.
  - Nonetheless, labor market indicators, on balance, show that underutilization of labor resources has diminished since early this year.
  - The Committee expects that, with appropriate policy accommodation, economic activity will expand at a moderate pace, with labor market indicators continuing to move toward levels the Committee judges consistent with its dual mandate.
  - The Committee continues to see the risks to the outlook for economic activity and the labor market as nearly balanced but is monitoring global economic and financial developments.
  - Inflation is anticipated to remain near its recent low level in the near term but the Committee expects inflation to rise gradually toward 2 percent over the medium term as the labor market improves further and the transitory effects of declines in energy and import prices dissipate.
  - To support continued progress toward maximum employment and price stability, the Committee today reaffirmed its view that the current 0 to 1/4 percent target range for the federal funds rate remains appropriate.
  - In determining whether it will be appropriate to raise the target range at its next meeting, the Committee will assess progress--both realized and expected--toward its objectives of maximum employment and 2 percent inflation.
  - The Committee anticipates that it will be appropriate to raise the target range for the federal funds rate when it has seen some further improvement in the labor market and is reasonably confident that inflation will move back to its 2 percent objective over the medium term.
  - When the Committee decides to begin to remove policy accommodation, it will take a balanced approach consistent with its longer-run goals of maximum employment and inflation of 2 percent.
  - The Committee currently anticipates that, even after employment and inflation are near mandate-consistent levels, economic conditions may, for some time, warrant keeping the target federal funds rate below levels the Committee views as normal in the longer run.
  + A range of recent labor market indicators, including ongoing job gains and declining unemployment, shows further improvement and confirms that underutilization of labor resources has diminished appreciably since early this year.
  + The Committee currently expects that, with gradual adjustments in the stance of monetary policy, economic activity will continue to expand at a moderate pace and labor market indicators will continue to strengthen.
  + Overall, taking into account domestic and international developments, the Committee sees the risks to the outlook for both economic activity and the labor market as balanced.
  + Inflation is expected to rise to 2 percent over the medium term as the transitory effects of declines in energy and import prices dissipate and the labor market strengthens further.
  + The Committee judges that there has been considerable improvement in labor market conditions this year, and it is reasonably confident that inflation will rise, over the medium term, to its 2 percent objective.
  + The stance of monetary policy remains accommodative after this increase, thereby supporting further improvement in labor market conditions and a return to 2 percent inflation.
  + In determining the timing and size of future adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will assess realized and expected economic conditions relative to its objectives of maximum employment and 2 percent inflation.

Policy language on this driver — unchanged context
  · Consistent with its statutory mandate, the Committee seeks to foster maximum employment and price stability.
  · This assessment will take into account a wide range of information, including measures of labor market conditions, indicators of inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and readings on financial and international developments.

raw_response: {"report": "The unemployment rate just printed a fresh 12-month low at 4.80%, down from 5.00% the prior two readings and continuing a clear downtrend: -0.20pp over 3 months, -0.40pp over 6 months, -0.90pp over 12 months. The series shows no reversals of consequence \u2014 every multi-horizon change is negative, and the rate sits at the 12-month low with zero distance above it (unrate_above_12m_low = 0). The Sahm-style gap is trivially small (+0.17pp) and irrelevant here since the signal is loosening-detection, not tightening; there is no loosening signal at all in this data. Policy language corroborates: the updated statement upgrades from \"labor market indicators...move toward\" to \"shows further improvement\" and \"diminished appreciably,\" and the Committee proceeded to raise rates, citing \"considerable improvement in labor market conditions.\" This is a textbook tightening trend with strengthening momentum, not deceleration \u2014 the pace of decline hasn't been decelerating (3m pace of -0.2/3mo is actually faster than the 6m average pace of -0.067/mo... roughly consistent). Level is still 0.8pp above the assumed 4% natural rate, leaving room for further tightening before convergence. Given the consistent multi-horizon negative trend, the fresh low, and corroborating policy language, I expect the unemployment rate to tick down or hold near this level next month, not rise.", "key_evidence": ["unrate_change_3m", "unrate_change_6m", "unrate_change_12m", "unrate_12m_low", "unrate_above_12m_low", "unrate_distance_from_natural"], "falsifier": "A monthly print showing unemployment rate rising back above 4.9-5.0%, or clear deceleration in job gains language reversing to \"diminished progress.\"", "missing_inputs": [], "direction": "down", "conviction": 0.55}

view.reasoning: The unemployment rate just printed a fresh 12-month low at 4.80%, down from 5.00% the prior two readings and continuing a clear downtrend: -0.20pp over 3 months, -0.40pp over 6 months, -0.90pp over 12 months. The series shows no reversals of consequence — every multi-horizon change is negative, and the rate sits at the 12-month low with zero distance above it (unrate_above_12m_low = 0). The Sahm-style gap is trivially small (+0.17pp) and irrelevant here since the signal is loosening-detection, not tightening; there is no loosening signal at all in this data. Policy language corroborates: the updated statement upgrades from "labor market indicators...move toward" to "shows further improvement" and "diminished appreciably," and the Committee proceeded to raise rates, citing "considerable improvement in labor market conditions." This is a textbook tightening trend with strengthening momentum, not deceleration — the pace of decline hasn't been decelerating (3m pace of -0.2/3mo is actually faster than the 6m average pace of -0.067/mo... roughly consistent). Level is still 0.8pp above the assumed 4% natural rate, leaving room for further tightening before convergence. Given the consistent multi-horizon negative trend, the fresh low, and corroborating policy language, I expect the unemployment rate to tick down or hold near this level next month, not rise.
view.report: The unemployment rate just printed a fresh 12-month low at 4.80%, down from 5.00% the prior two readings and continuing a clear downtrend: -0.20pp over 3 months, -0.40pp over 6 months, -0.90pp over 12 months. The series shows no reversals of consequence — every multi-horizon change is negative, and the rate sits at the 12-month low with zero distance above it (unrate_above_12m_low = 0). The Sahm-style gap is trivially small (+0.17pp) and irrelevant here since the signal is loosening-detection, not tightening; there is no loosening signal at all in this data. Policy language corroborates: the updated statement upgrades from "labor market indicators...move toward" to "shows further improvement" and "diminished appreciably," and the Committee proceeded to raise rates, citing "considerable improvement in labor market conditions." This is a textbook tightening trend with strengthening momentum, not deceleration — the pace of decline hasn't been decelerating (3m pace of -0.2/3mo is actually faster than the 6m average pace of -0.067/mo... roughly consistent). Level is still 0.8pp above the assumed 4% natural rate, leaving room for further tightening before convergence. Given the consistent multi-horizon negative trend, the fresh low, and corroborating policy language, I expect the unemployment rate to tick down or hold near this level next month, not rise.
view.falsifier: A monthly print showing unemployment rate rising back above 4.9-5.0%, or clear deceleration in job gains language reversing to "diminished progress."

raw_response: {"report": "The last print ticked up to 4.90% from a 12-month low of 4.80%, breaking a string of new lows and validating the risk I flagged last time (rising back above ~4.9-5.0%). That said, this is a single-month uptick of just 0.10pp against a 12-month decline of 0.60pp and a 3-month/6-month change still net -0.20pp \u2014 the broader trend remains one of gradual tightening (falling unemployment), not a reversal. The Sahm-style gap (current vs 12m low) is only 0.10pp, far below levels that would signal a genuine turn. Range position is low (0.13), meaning the rate sits near the bottom of its 12-month range, and distance from the ~4% natural rate is still meaningfully positive at 0.90pp, meaning slack has room to keep shrinking. Policy language continues to affirm \"further improvement,\" \"additional decline in underutilization,\" and \"considerable improvement in labor market conditions\" \u2014 no language suggesting stalling or reversal. Given one soft print doesn't overturn a still-intact downtrend, but the tick-up is real evidence of possible flattening in the pace of decline, I read this as a stabilization/pause rather than continued sustained decline. I'll shift toward flat-to-slightly-down, tempering prior conviction given the miss.", "key_evidence": ["unrate_change_12m", "unrate_12m_low", "sahm_gap", "unrate_range_position", "unrate_distance_from_natural", "unrate last two observations (4.80 -> 4.90)"], "falsifier": "A print rising further to 5.0%+ with sahm_gap widening past 0.3-0.5pp, or policy language shifting from \"improvement\" to \"diminished progress.\"", "missing_inputs": [], "direction": "flat", "conviction": 0.35}

view.reasoning: The last print ticked up to 4.90% from a 12-month low of 4.80%, breaking a string of new lows and validating the risk I flagged last time (rising back above ~4.9-5.0%). That said, this is a single-month uptick of just 0.10pp against a 12-month decline of 0.60pp and a 3-month/6-month change still net -0.20pp — the broader trend remains one of gradual tightening (falling unemployment), not a reversal. The Sahm-style gap (current vs 12m low) is only 0.10pp, far below levels that would signal a genuine turn. Range position is low (0.13), meaning the rate sits near the bottom of its 12-month range, and distance from the ~4% natural rate is still meaningfully positive at 0.90pp, meaning slack has room to keep shrinking. Policy language continues to affirm "further improvement," "additional decline in underutilization," and "considerable improvement in labor market conditions" — no language suggesting stalling or reversal. Given one soft print doesn't overturn a still-intact downtrend, but the tick-up is real evidence of possible flattening in the pace of decline, I read this as a stabilization/pause rather than continued sustained decline. I'll shift toward flat-to-slightly-down, tempering prior conviction given the miss.
view.report: The last print ticked up to 4.90% from a 12-month low of 4.80%, breaking a string of new lows and validating the risk I flagged last time (rising back above ~4.9-5.0%). That said, this is a single-month uptick of just 0.10pp against a 12-month decline of 0.60pp and a 3-month/6-month change still net -0.20pp — the broader trend remains one of gradual tightening (falling unemployment), not a reversal. The Sahm-style gap (current vs 12m low) is only 0.10pp, far below levels that would signal a genuine turn. Range position is low (0.13), meaning the rate sits near the bottom of its 12-month range, and distance from the ~4% natural rate is still meaningfully positive at 0.90pp, meaning slack has room to keep shrinking. Policy language continues to affirm "further improvement," "additional decline in underutilization," and "considerable improvement in labor market conditions" — no language suggesting stalling or reversal. Given one soft print doesn't overturn a still-intact downtrend, but the tick-up is real evidence of possible flattening in the pace of decline, I read this as a stabilization/pause rather than continued sustained decline. I'll shift toward flat-to-slightly-down, tempering prior conviction given the miss.
view.falsifier: A print rising further to 5.0%+ with sahm_gap widening past 0.3-0.5pp, or policy language shifting from "improvement" to "diminished progress."

view.reasoning: The prior call (further decline) was wrong: unemployment ticked up from 4.00% to 4.10%, a one-tick reversal off the 12-month low. That said, the broader trend remains one of tightening, not loosening: unrate_change_3m (-0.10pp), change_6m (-0.30pp), and change_12m (-0.50pp) are all still negative, and the level (4.10%) sits only 0.10pp above the 12-month low and 0.10pp below the assumed natural rate of ~4.2% region (distance_from_natural +0.10pp, meaning we're still tight). The Sahm-style gap is a trivial +0.07pp, far from any recessionary signal threshold, so this single-month uptick looks like noise around a still-tight labor market rather than a genuine turn. Policy language reinforces this: the FOMC statement affirms labor market "continued to strengthen," "unemployment rate has stayed low," and "conditions will remain strong" — consistent with tightness, not a loosening regime. Given one month of contrary movement in a series that had been grinding down for a year, I don't have enough evidence yet to call another leg down, but I also don't see confirmation of a genuine reversal. I'll call this roughly flat with a slight tightening bias, tempered by the miss last time.

Given the level is already at the cycle low and momentum has been consistently negative (falling) over multiple horizons, the most likely near-term outcome is another flat-to-down print, not a reversal upward. I lean toward "down" with modest conviction, since the series could also plateau at 4.0% given limited room to fall further before natural-rate constraints bind.

view.reasoning: The unemployment rate held at 3.80% for the third consecutive reading, matching my prior flat call and confirming it was correct — no print above 3.90% or below 3.70% materialized, so neither loosening nor renewed tightening momentum is evident. The 3m and 6m changes are both exactly 0.00pp, indicating a genuine plateau. The 12m change of -0.40pp still reflects tightening over the past year, but that move has fully stalled in recent months. The Sahm-rule style gap (current vs 12m low) is a trivial +0.07pp, far below any threshold suggesting acceleration in labor market slack. Range position at 0.25 shows the rate sits closer to its 12-month low than high, consistent with a still-tight labor market that is simply no longer tightening further. The level itself, at 0.20pp below my assumed natural rate of ~4%, points to a tight market, but the flat recent trend dominates my forward call since the mandate weighs both level and momentum. raw_response: {"report": "The prior call (up, 0.45) badly understated what happened: unemployment exploded from 3.50% to 4.40% and then to 14.80% in the latest print \u2014 an unprecedented, sudden collapse in labor market tightness. This is not a gradual Sahm-rule-style deterioration; it is a shock discontinuity (12m change +11.1pp, sahm_gap +4.07pp, range_position at 1.0, distance from natural rate +10.8pp). Such a violent, non-linear jump strongly suggests a mass layoff/shutdown-type event rather than typical business-cycle loosening. For the next monthly report, the key question is whether this level persists, extends further, or partially reverses. Historically, shock-driven unemployment spikes of this magnitude tend to keep rising for at least one more reading before stabilizing or reversing, as labor market disruptions typically take multiple months to fully register and any recovery is gradual. Given the sheer velocity and magnitude of this move, and no evidence yet of stabilization, I lean toward further deterioration or at best a plateau at this elevated level. The extreme jump makes further increase somewhat more likely than an immediate reversal, though the magnitude of continued increase is highly uncertain.", "key_evidence": "unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_low", "falsifier": "A next print back toward 8-10% or lower would signal rapid labor market recovery; a print above 15-16% would confirm continued severe deterioration.", "missing_inputs": [{"driver": "financial_conditions", "why": "Whether credit/financial stress is intensifying would indicate if the labor shock is deepening or stabilizing."}, {"driver": "balance_sheet", "why": "Scale of policy support could affect how quickly firms rehire or continue layoffs."}], "direction": "up", "conviction": 0.35}

view.reasoning: The prior call (up, 0.45) badly understated what happened: unemployment exploded from 3.50% to 4.40% and then to 14.80% in the latest print — an unprecedented, sudden collapse in labor market tightness. Such a violent, non-linear jump strongly suggests a mass layoff/shutdown-type event rather than typical business-cycle loosening. For the next monthly report, the key question is whether this level persists, extends further, or partially reverses. Historically, shock-driven unemployment spikes of this magnitude tend to keep rising for at least one more reading before stabilizing or reversing, as labor market disruptions typically take multiple months to fully register and any recovery is gradual. raw_response: {"report": "The last print shows unemployment falling from 14.80% to 13.20%, a 1.6pp improvement after the historic COVID-driven spike from a 3.5% baseline. The level remains extraordinarily elevated versus the ~4% natural rate (distance_from_natural +9.2pp, sahm_gap +7.3pp), so the labor market is still extremely loose by any historical standard. However, the direction this month is down, not up \u2014 a meaningful decline from the peak. Given the extreme volatility just witnessed (job losses of this magnitude are unprecedented in this series), and with policy language still describing an unfolding crisis with \"considerable risks,\" it's plausible the rate could still move either direction sharply. But the immediate print-over-print momentum has turned downward from the 14.8 peak, and reopening-type dynamics after an acute shock often produce further partial reversals in the following month. view.reasoning: The last print fell to 8.40% from 10.20%, continuing the sharp post-pandemic-shock recovery trajectory (14.8% peak → 13.2% → 11.0% → 10.2% → 8.4%). Policy language shift also confirms this: from describing "sharp declines and surge in job losses" to "activity and employment have picked up somewhat in recent months," acknowledging the improvement while noting levels remain well below pre-shock. Momentum here has been consistently downward for four straight readings with large magnitude moves, and nothing in the data suggests a reversal. view.reasoning: The previous call (down/0.55) was correct: unemployment fell from 8.40% to 7.80%, continuing the sharp post-spike recovery from the 14.80% peak. The trend remains a clear, rapid improvement: -3.20pp over 3 months, though still +4.30pp above the 12-month low and +3.80pp above the natural rate of ~4%. The pace of improvement has been very fast (3.5→14.8→7.8), consistent with a mechanical snapback in job losses being reversed as activity resumes, and policy language now explicitly notes activity and employment "have picked up somewhat in recent months." There is no evidence of stalling or reversal in the data — each of the last five prints has declined monotonically (14.8, 13.2, 11.0, 10.2, 8.4, 7.8). Given the strength and consistency of this recovery trajectory, and no sign of deceleration in the pace of decline, I expect the unemployment rate to continue falling at the next report, though the magnitude of monthly improvement may moderate as the rate approaches more normal levels. I maintain a moderate-to-high conviction the rate will be lower next month, tempered by the still-large distance from the natural rate and by the inherent uncertainty in how quickly a snapback recovery decelerates.

The trend remains a clear, rapid improvement: -3.20pp over 3 months, though still +4.30pp above the 12-month low and +3.80pp above the natural rate of ~4%. The pace of improvement has been very fast (3.5→14.8→7.8), consistent with a mechanical snapback in job losses being reversed as activity resumes, and policy language now explicitly notes activity and employment "have picked up somewhat in recent months." There is no evidence of stalling or reversal in the data — each of the last five prints has declined monotonically (14.8, 13.2, 11.0, 10.2, 8.4, 7.8). Given the strength and consistency of this recovery trajectory, and no sign of deceleration in the pace of decline, I expect the unemployment rate to continue falling at the next report, though the magnitude of monthly improvement may moderate as the rate approaches more normal levels. I maintain a moderate-to-high conviction the rate will be lower next month, tempered by the still-large distance from the natural rate and by the inherent uncertainty in how quickly a snapback recovery decelerates.

view.reasoning: The latest print of 3.60% is a new 12-month low, undercutting the prior floor of 3.70% and confirming that my previous "flat" call was wrong — the market resumed tightening rather than stabilizing. The unemployment rate has now fallen for three straight readings (3.70, 3.70, 3.60) after a brief uptick to 3.90-4.00%, and the 3m/6m/12m changes are all negative (-0.30, -0.50, -2.20 pp), showing sustained downward momentum in unemployment. The level is 0.40pp below the ~4% natural rate estimate, and the Sahm-style gap (current vs 12m low) is a negligible +0.07pp, meaning we are sitting essentially at the cycle low with no sign of the sharp-rise pattern that would signal loosening. Policy language reinforces this, upgrading "strong" job gains to "robust" and reiterating substantial declines in unemployment. There is no evidence in the measurements of any turn — no rise off the low, no deceleration in the pace of decline. Given the consistent multi-horizon negative changes and a fresh cycle low, the near-term signal favors continued tightness or at worst stabilization at a very low level. I'll lean toward another flat-to-slightly-down reading next month, but with the recognition that at levels this low, mean-reversion risk rises.

My prior flat call was essentially correct: the rate did not drop back toward 4.0% nor did it break clearly above 4.2%, it simply held there. Policy language still describes conditions as "stabilized at a low level" and "solid," and the shift to "risks roughly in balance" (from previous asymmetric framing) suggests the committee sees less urgency in labor slack, consistent with a plateau rather than acceleration. Given three consecutive flat readings at the high end of the range, with no confirmed break higher and no sign of reversal lower, the most defensible call remains flat, though the persistence at the 12-month high with sahm_gap of 0.30 keeps some modest upside risk in play.

raw_response: {"report": "The latest print fell to 4.40% from 4.50%, a decline of 0.10pp \u2014 this is the \"below 4.60% acceleration threshold\" and close to my stated stabilization marker (4.30% or below), though it didn't quite reach that level. My prior \"up\" call was directionally too aggressive for this print; the rate actually ticked down last month rather than continuing to rise. That said, the broader trend over 12 months is still up (+0.20pp), the 3-month change is still positive (+0.10pp), and the level remains 0.40pp above the 12-month low and 0.40pp above the natural rate estimate, with the Sahm gap holding at 0.43pp \u2014 closer to conventional recession-signal territory than to full stabilization. Policy language changes are notable: officials now describe downside employment risks as having \"risen in recent months\" (upgraded from a static risk assessment), and note \"more recent indicators are consistent with\" the slowing/upward-edging narrative \u2014 suggesting they see continued softening beneath the single-month dip. Given the single-month decline could be noise around a still-elevated and drifting-up base, and the policy tone leans toward further loosening, I lean toward another modest uptick or at least sideways-to-up next report, but with reduced conviction since the immediate print surprised to the downside.", "key_evidence": ["unrate_change_12m", "unrate_change_3m", "sahm_gap", "unrate_above_12m_low", "unrate_distance_from_natural", "unrate_range_position"], "falsifier": "A print at or below 4.30% would confirm stabilization/reversal; a print above 4.60% would confirm renewed sharp acceleration.", "missing_inputs": [], "direction": "up", "conviction": 0.3}

view.reasoning: The latest print fell to 4.40% from 4.50%, a decline of 0.10pp — this is the "below 4.60% acceleration threshold" and close to my stated stabilization marker (4.30% or below), though it didn't quite reach that level. My prior "up" call was directionally too aggressive for this print; the rate actually ticked down last month rather than continuing to rise. That said, the broader trend over 12 months is still up (+0.20pp), the 3-month change is still positive (+0.10pp), and the level remains 0.40pp above the 12-month low and 0.40pp above the natural rate estimate, with the Sahm gap holding at 0.43pp — closer to conventional recession-signal territory than to full stabilization. Policy language changes are notable: officials now describe downside employment risks as having "risen in recent months" (upgraded from a static risk assessment), and note "more recent indicators are consistent with" the slowing/upward-edging narrative — suggesting they see continued softening beneath the single-month dip. Given the single-month decline could be noise around a still-elevated and drifting-up base, and the policy tone leans toward further loosening, I lean toward another modest uptick or at least sideways-to-up next report, but with reduced conviction since the immediate print surprised to the downside.
